title: The Household Debt Analogy Is a Political Weapon, Not an Economic Argument

source.original: Where Does Money Come From? — Josh Ryan-Collins, Tony Greenham, Richard Werner, Andrew Jackson

body_html:
Politicians across the political spectrum repeat one analogy so often that it has achieved the status of common sense: a government must manage its finances like a household. You can't spend more than you earn. Running up debt burdens future generations. Fiscal responsibility means balancing the books. The analogy is intuitive, rhetorically powerful, and wrong in ways that have caused enormous, measurable harm to real populations through unnecessary austerity programs.

The household analogy breaks down because governments that issue their own currency are not revenue-constrained in the same way households are. A household cannot create the money it needs to pay its debts. A government that borrows in its own currency — which it has the sovereign authority to issue — faces a fundamentally different set of constraints. The relevant limits are not accounting limits but real-resource limits: a government cannot spend beyond the economy's productive capacity without generating inflation. That is a real constraint. But it is categorically different from a household's inability to pay the mortgage.

The mechanics matter here. When a government runs a deficit, it issues bonds — pieces of paper that represent future claims on tax revenue. Commercial banks and pension funds buy these bonds, exchanging existing bank reserves for government securities. This is not money creation in the way that bank lending is money creation. The government does not manufacture new purchasing power when it borrows; it is drawing on purchasing power that already exists in the system. The crowding out hypothesis — that government borrowing competes with private investment for a fixed pool of savings, driving up interest rates — fails because banks create credit based on demand and profitability, not from a pre-existing pool. There is no fixed pot of savings to crowd out.

The Eurozone is the clearest real-world test of what happens when the household analogy is embedded in institutional architecture. The Maastricht Treaty and the Stability and Growth Pact codify deficit and debt limits as if the analogy were literally true. Peripheral Eurozone countries during the 2010s sovereign debt crisis — Greece most dramatically — could not monetize their debts because they had surrendered monetary sovereignty when they adopted the euro. The ECB was constitutionally prohibited from directly financing member state governments. The result was forced austerity applied to economies in depression, which deepened the depression, which made the debt ratios worse, which justified more austerity. The logic of the household analogy, built into treaty law, produced a decade of unnecessary suffering.

Japan offers the counterexample. The Japanese government has run persistent deficits for three decades. Its debt-to-GDP ratio exceeded 260% — the highest among major economies. According to the household analogy, Japan should have faced a debt crisis, a currency collapse, and soaring interest rates long ago. Instead, Japan has had near-zero interest rates, a stable yen, and deflation has been the persistent problem rather than inflation. Japan's experience is difficult to reconcile with the household model and much easier to understand through the lens of monetary sovereignty and the endogenous money framework.

The political function of the household analogy is worth naming explicitly. It naturalizes public spending constraints that are, in fact, political choices. When austerity is presented as fiscal necessity rather than ideology, it forecloses debate about distribution — who bears the cost of deficit reduction, and who benefits. Understanding the actual mechanics of government finance does not resolve those distributional debates, but it moves them from the realm of false necessity into the realm of genuine political choice. That is a significant shift. The question stops being "can we afford this?" and becomes "who do we want to tax and who do we want to benefit?" Those are harder conversations. They are also the right ones.
